era_context: The Western Roman Empire is in terminal decline. The Visigothic kingdom under Alaric I is consolidating power in the Balkans and preparing to invade Italy. In the East, the Byzantine Empire under Theodosius II maintains stronger institutional continuity. The Sassanid Persian Empire dominates the Middle East. In China, the Liu Song dynasty has recently been established (420 CE is imminent); the Northern and Southern dynasties period is fragmenting the classical Han cultural sphere. Germanic migrations are reshaping European political geography.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Roman frontier (archaeological evidence sparse)

events.title: Radagaisus and the Germanic invasion of Italy
events.description: The Gothic general Radagaisus, commanding a confederation of Germanic tribes (Goths, Vandals, Alans, and others estimated at 20,000–200,000 warriors depending on source), invaded the Roman Empire across the Danube and penetrated into Italy. Roman general Stilicho intercepted and defeated Radagaisus at the Battle of Faesulae (near modern Florence) in August 405. Radagaisus was captured and executed. This invasion demonstrated the fragility of Roman frontier defenses and the scale of Germanic pressure on the empire's borders. The defeat temporarily stabilized the Western Empire but did not resolve underlying military or economic crises.

sources.name: Orosius, Historiae adversum paganos (early 5th century chronicle)
Zosimus, Historia Nova (6th century Byzantine historian)
Claudian, De bello Gothico (contemporary panegyric poem)

events.certainty_note: The invasion and battle are attested in multiple near-contemporary and later sources. Claudian's poem was written shortly after the events. Exact casualty figures and tribal composition are debated, but the core event is well-established.
cross_references: 410-001 (Alaric's sack of Rome, continuation of Gothic pressure)

events.title: Vandal and Suevic crossing of the Rhine
events.description: On the last day of 406 CE (or possibly 405 CE depending on source tradition), Germanic tribes—primarily Vandals, Sueves, and Alans—crossed the frozen Rhine River into Roman Gaul, breaching one of the empire's most critical frontiers. This crossing marked the beginning of the end of Roman control in Gaul and initiated a cascade of migrations that would reshape Western Europe. The crossing was facilitated by the withdrawal of Roman troops to deal with Radagaisus's invasion in Italy and internal civil conflicts. The tribes subsequently ravaged Gaul for several years before moving into Hispania (Spain) around 409 CE.
key_figures: Vandal and Suevic tribal leaders (names largely unrecorded in surviving sources)
events.certainty_note: The Rhine crossing is attested in multiple sources, but the exact date is disputed between late 406 and 405. Most modern scholarship places it in late 406, but it is included here as a significant event of the 405–406 transition period. The sources are all later compilations, not contemporary records.

events.title: Theodosius II assumes sole rule of Eastern Roman Empire
events.description: Theodosius II, aged approximately 13, became the sole reigning emperor of the Eastern Roman Empire following the death of his father Arcadius (395 CE) and the regency period under his sister Pulcheria and the praetorian prefect Anthemius. By 405, Theodosius II had consolidated personal authority, though Pulcheria remained influential. His reign (408–450 CE) would be marked by relative stability in the East, the construction of the Theodosian Walls around Constantinople, and the codification of Roman law (the Theodosian Code, 438 CE). His reign contrasts sharply with the chaos of the Western Empire during this period.

sources.name: Theodoret of Cyrus, Ecclesiastical History (5th century)

events.certainty_note: Theodosius II's reign dates and consolidation of power are well-documented in Byzantine sources. The transition from regency to personal rule occurred gradually; 405 CE marks a point of increasing personal authority.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of the Rhine crossing (405 vs. 406) is disputed in modern scholarship. Most recent historians place it in late 406 rather than 405, though some sources conflate the two years. The scale of Radagaisus's invasion force is highly uncertain; ancient sources give figures ranging from 20,000 to 200,000 warriors, and modern estimates vary widely. Some scholars argue that the 'invasion' was partly a negotiated settlement rather than purely military conquest. The significance of these events for 'decline' narratives has been questioned by recent scholarship, which emphasizes continuity and transformation rather than catastrophic collapse.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 405 CE is dominated by Roman and Byzantine sources, creating a Western/Eastern Mediterranean bias. Germanic tribal perspectives are entirely absent from surviving written sources; our understanding of Germanic motivations and internal politics relies on Roman characterizations and later reconstruction. The sources are predominantly later compilations (Orosius, Zosimus, Hydatius) rather than contemporary records, introducing potential distortions. Claudian's panegyric on Stilicho is contemporary but is a political document designed to glorify the general, not an objective historical account. Sub-Saharan African, East Asian, and Central Asian events of this year are essentially absent from Western historical records.

graph_edges.note: Stilicho's victory over Radagaisus temporarily stabilized the Western Empire, but his subsequent fall and Alaric's invasion of Italy followed within five years.
The Rhine crossing initiated a chain of migrations that culminated in Vandal and Suevic entry into Hispania.
Both events represent concurrent Germanic pressure on Roman frontiers, likely related to broader migration patterns.
